title: Te Hauora o Te Awa: The Health of Our River / Te Hauora o Te Awa: Te Oranga o tō tātou Awa
subject: Health & Physical Education
culturalContext: Students will explore the connection between human wellbeing and the health of Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku (the Mangakōtukutuku Stream), a significant waterway flowing through our rohe that holds cultural importance for Ngāti Kahungunu as a source of mahinga kai (traditional food gathering) and spiritual significance. The lesson connects to the story of Rongomaiwahine, whose descendants are kaitiaki of these waters.
learningObjectives: Conduct and record basic water quality tests (pH, clarity, temperature) at two points along Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku
Explain how the mauri (life force) of the awa impacts the physical, mental, and spiritual wellbeing of the local community
Create a simple action plan proposing one practical way to improve or maintain the health of our local waterway
Present findings using basic te reo Māori terms related to water health (awa, mauri, wai māori, kaitiakitanga)

activities.title: He Whakawhiti Kōrero: Introduction to Our Awa
activities.description: Begin with a karakia to acknowledge the awa. Students view historical and contemporary photos of Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku and listen to a short audio recording from a local kaumātua discussing the traditional and current uses of the awa and its importance to hauora. Students brainstorm initial ideas about how a healthy/unhealthy river might affect people's wellbeing.

activities.title: He Haerenga: Awa Field Investigation
activities.description: Guided by the kaiako and a representative from the local marae committee, students visit two accessible sites on Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku (e.g., one upstream near a reserve, one downstream nearer to urban development). In small groups, they use provided kits to conduct and record water tests (temperature, pH, visual clarity). They also make observational notes about the surrounding environment and any signs of human impact.

activities.title: He Wānanga: Analysing the Mauri of the Awa
activities.description: Back in the classroom, groups collate their field data and observations. Using a simplified 'Mauri Model' diagram (healthy/compromised/degraded), they discuss and map the potential state of the awa's mauri at each site. They then explore the direct and indirect links between the health of the awa and human health (e.g., safe swimming, mahinga kai, mental wellbeing from connection to place).

activities.title: He Mahi Ngātahi: Kaitiakitanga Action Planning
activities.description: Drawing on their analysis, each group brainstorms one realistic action the class or college could take to demonstrate kaitiakitanga towards Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku (e.g., designing posters to discourage littering, planning a native planting session with the enviro-group, creating a simple public information flyer). They draft a basic plan outlining their idea.

resources: Hanna Instruments HI98118 pHep®5 pH/Temperature Tester or similar simple pH meters
Local kaumātua or representative from Tamatea Marae (for cultural guidance and storytelling)
Hawke's Bay Regional Council website - 'State of the Environment' reporting and water quality data
Book: 'Te Mātauranga o te Wai - Māori Perspectives on Water' (relevant excerpts provided by kaiako)

assessment.type: Formative - Group Presentation and Action Plan Outline
tasks: Present key findings from your field investigation to the class.
Explain your group's assessment of the mauri at your two sites using your data and observations.
Describe your proposed kaitiakitanga action and how it connects to protecting community hauora.
Use at least three of the following te reo Māori terms correctly: awa, mauri, wai māori, kaitiaki, hauora.

nzcAlignment: Health and Physical Education: Strand A - Personal Health and Physical Development (A3 - Safety Management); Strand D - Healthy Communities and Environments (D1 - Societal Attitudes and Values; D2 - Community Resources; D3 - Rights, Responsibilities, and Laws; D4 - People and the Environment)
Science: Nature of Science - Participating and Contributing; Planet Earth and Beyond - Earth Systems
